items.q: Is a price cut always the best way to raise revenue?
items.correct: Only if demand is price elastic — if demand is inelastic, cutting price actually lowers total revenue; elasticity should guide pricing decisions.
items.misconception: Lower prices always mean more revenue.
items.random_wrong: Firms must get government permission to change prices.
items.why: Linking pricing decisions to price elasticity is the core analytical skill of A-level marketing.

items.q: Does a strong brand only matter for charging higher prices?
items.correct: No — brands also build loyalty and repeat purchase, reduce the price elasticity of demand, support new product launches and cut promotion costs over time.
items.misconception: Branding is just a logo that lets firms overcharge.
items.random_wrong: Brands are licences issued by trading standards.
items.why: Students reduce brand value to a price premium, missing loyalty and brand-extension benefits.

items.q: Are the 7Ps equally important for every business?
items.correct: No — the right emphasis depends on the product and market: people and process dominate in services, while product and place may dominate for physical goods.
items.misconception: A good marketing mix gives equal weight to all seven Ps.
items.random_wrong: The 7Ps must be applied in alphabetical order.
items.why: Application marks come from tailoring the mix to context, not reciting the list.

items.q: Which of the following is part of the extended 7 Ps but NOT the original 4 Ps?
items.why: Correct answer: Physical evidence. “Price” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which P covers advertising and public relations?
items.why: It's Promotion. “Place” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many Ps in the extended marketing mix?
items.why: Exact answer: 7. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
